title: The Interwar Period
summary: The twenty years between the two World Wars — a period of democratic experimentation, fascist and communist revolution, economic catastrophe, and the failure of the international order created at Versailles — that ended with the most destructive war in human history.
hook: The interwar period began with the most ambitious attempt to create a peaceful international order in modern history and ended with the most catastrophic failure of that attempt. Twenty years separated the Treaty of Versailles from the German invasion of Poland. Within that time, democracy collapsed across most of Europe, the global economy imploded, and the conditions for genocide and world war were assembled piece by piece.

beginner.thePeriod:
In 1925, the leaders of Europe signed a treaty and shook hands, and many people believed the great wars were over for good. Crowds celebrated. Newspapers wrote about a new age of peace. Fourteen years later, German tanks rolled into Poland and one of the deadliest wars in history began.

Those years between the two World Wars are called the interwar period. They ran from 1919 to 1939. Looking back, the path to disaster can seem obvious, as if it had to happen.

It did not have to happen. The 1920s held real hope and real calm. The 1930s offered other choices at almost every turn. People made decisions, and different decisions were possible. The catastrophe was built step by step, not fixed from the start.
beginner.theBoundaries:
The period opens in 1919, when leaders met in Paris to end the First World War. They signed several treaties, and the best known of them was the Treaty of Versailles, which redrew the map of Europe. The period closes on September 1, 1939, when Germany invaded Poland.

Historians often split these years into two halves. The 1920s brought recovery and a hopeful mood. The 1930s brought collapse.

But the simple story of happy twenties and grim thirties only fits some countries. Germany suffered terrible money troubles in the early 1920s, when savings became worthless almost overnight. Much of Eastern Europe stayed poor and shaky the whole decade. The true turning point came in 1931, when a banking crisis in Germany turned an American stock market crash into a disaster across Europe.
beginner.theConditions:
Four crises tangled together to shape these years.

The first was a crisis of government. One country after another gave up on democracy. By 1939, only a handful of European nations still let their people vote freely and choose their leaders.

The second was money. After 1929, the Great Depression threw millions out of work. Governments that could not fix this lost the trust of their people. Movements that promised jobs and pride, including violent ones, grew fast.

The third was a crisis of safety. The League of Nations was built to stop wars. But it could not stop Japan, Italy, or Germany when they seized land by force.

The fourth was a battle of ideas. Three ways of running a country competed: democracy, fascism, and communism. Each claimed to own the future. For much of the 1930s, democracy looked like the side that was losing.
beginner.internalDiversity:
The interwar years were not all gloom. The 1920s burst with new art and music. Jazz spread across the world. Writers, filmmakers, and painters created bold new work. In the United States, Black artists and writers in Harlem built a powerful new culture.

Life also looked very different depending on where you lived. Women won the right to vote in many countries. Workers gained new rights. Some nations built early versions of the welfare state, where the government helps people who are old, sick, or out of work.

The United States stayed a democracy. Britain and France did too, even through hard times. The Scandinavian countries built systems of care that lasted long after the period ended. The disaster was real, but it was not the whole story.
beginner.longConsequences:
What happened in these years shaped the world that came after.

The failure of the League of Nations led to a stronger plan: the United Nations. The pain of the Depression pushed governments to promise jobs for everyone and to manage their economies more carefully. The horror of fascism led the winners to tie West Germany closely to its neighbors, so that it could not threaten them on its own again.

The period also taught lessons that leaders still repeat. One lesson stands out: giving in to a bully does not buy peace, it invites more demands. This idea has been used to argue for wars ever since. Sometimes it fit the situation well. Sometimes leaders forced it onto a problem where it did not belong, and it led them astray.
beginner.periodizationDebate:
The big argument is simple to state. Was the disaster bound to happen, or could it have been avoided?

One view treats the whole period as an age of catastrophe. On this account, the peace built in 1919 was broken from the start, and it was sure to produce fascism and war in the end. The system was doomed.

The other view disagrees. It says the 1920s held real chances for a stable, peaceful Europe. The disaster came from specific mistakes and choices, not from fate. Germany might have stayed a democracy. Giving in to Hitler was a decision, not a trap with no exit.

Many historians now lean toward the second view. The Depression was not guaranteed. The League could have been built better. Understanding this period means seeing the moments when things could have gone another way.

general.thePeriod:
In October 1925, European statesmen gathered at the Swiss town of Locarno and pledged to settle their disputes peacefully. The mood was euphoric. For a few years afterward, a stable and reconciled Europe seemed genuinely within reach. Less than fifteen years later, that Europe lay in ruins as a second world war began.

The interwar period runs from the peace treaties of 1919 to Germany's invasion of Poland in 1939. Seen from 1945, it can look like a straight road to catastrophe, with every step leading toward the abyss. That impression is partly a trick of hindsight. The 1920s contained real stability and real hope, and the 1930s contained real alternatives at almost every decision point. Recovering those open possibilities is the central task of understanding the period. The disaster was assembled through choices, not delivered by fate.
general.theBoundaries:
How the 1920s relate to the 1930s is the key question of where to draw the period's internal lines. The familiar picture pairs the 'Roaring Twenties' of prosperity with the Depression-stricken 1930s. That picture is geographically narrow. The boom was mostly American and Western European. Germany, Austria, and much of Eastern Europe lived through severe instability across the 1920s.

German hyperinflation wiped out middle-class savings in 1923, and Weimar politics lurched from one crisis to the next. Rural Eastern Europe stayed depressed for most of the decade. So the cheerful image of the twenties was always partial.

The Wall Street Crash of 1929 is usually named as the turning point, but its damage took time to reach Europe. The decisive moment came with the German banking collapse of 1931. That was when an American financial shock became a European political emergency, toppling governments and clearing the path for extremism.
general.theConditions:
Four crises wound together to define the period, and the economic one did much to drive the rest. The Depression after 1929 was unusually deep and unusually global, and much of that severity traces to the gold standard, the system that tied currencies to fixed amounts of gold. Staying on gold forced governments to cut spending and let prices fall rather than ease the pain, and it spread the contraction from country to country. Nations that abandoned gold early, such as Britain in 1931, tended to recover sooner. This monetary reading has become the standard explanation for why the slump bit so hard.

Mass unemployment then became the central political fact of the 1930s. Governments that could not relieve it lost legitimacy, and movements promising work and national renewal gained ground. Alongside this ran a crisis of collective security, as the League of Nations proved unable to halt aggression by Japan, Italy, and Germany. Underneath it all was a contest of ideologies, with liberal democracy, fascism, and communism each claiming to embody the future.
general.internalDiversity:
The interwar years were one of the richest creative moments in modern Western culture. Modernism reshaped literature and painting, jazz crossed the Atlantic, and cinema became a mass art. The Harlem Renaissance gave African American culture a powerful new voice. Radio, meanwhile, transformed political life, handing leaders a direct line into millions of homes and giving demagogues a potent new instrument.

The period's politics were just as varied as its art. The Spanish Civil War, fought from 1936 to 1939, distilled the era's central conflict into a single arena, pitting fascism against a coalition of communists, socialists, and liberals. Its outcome, a victory for the right under Franco, felt to many at the time like a rehearsal for a wider European defeat. The writer George Orwell, who fought there and was wounded, left one of the most vivid accounts of how intoxicating and how bitter that struggle became.
general.longConsequences:
The lessons drawn from these years have shaped international politics ever since. The 'Munich lesson', taken from the 1938 agreement that failed to satisfy Hitler, holds that conceding to an aggressive dictator invites further aggression rather than buying peace. It has been invoked to justify a long line of Western military actions, from Korea to Iraq. The analogy has sometimes clarified a genuine threat and sometimes been forced onto situations it did not fit, foreclosing options that deserved a hearing.

The economic lessons were absorbed more cleanly. The postwar Bretton Woods system was designed to avoid the deflationary traps of the gold standard. The commitment to full employment that marked postwar governments grew directly out of the political damage that mass joblessness had done. And the drive toward European integration was partly an effort to make another continental war unthinkable by binding former enemies into shared institutions and intertwined economies.
general.periodizationDebate:
The history of appeasement has been among the most contested fields in twentieth-century scholarship. An early and damning view, captured by the wartime pamphlet 'Guilty Men', treated Neville Chamberlain's policy as a culpable failure that could have been avoided. A later wave of historians pushed back, arguing that appeasement was a rational response to real constraints: British military weakness, reluctant Commonwealth partners, a war-weary public, and genuine uncertainty about how far Hitler meant to go.

More recent work has tried to hold both truths together. It accepts that the constraints were real and that appeasement was understandable in the moment. But it also restores room for moral judgment, noting that those very constraints were partly the product of earlier choices. Decisions made in the 1920s and early 1930s narrowed the options available later, which returns the debate to its deepest question. Historians still disagree about how far the catastrophe was built into the situation and how far it was made by the people who governed within it.

advanced.thePeriod: Scholarly accounts of the interwar period have been transformed by the opening of national archives across Europe and by the rise of transnational and global history approaches that situate European developments within broader international contexts. The 'new diplomatic history' — associated with scholars including Steiner, MacMillan, and Patrick Cohrs — recovers the genuine possibilities for stable international order in the 1920s while analyzing why they were not realized. Cohrs's The Unfinished Peace After World War I (2006) argues that the 1920s saw genuine attempts to build a durable peace that failed not because of Versailles's injustice but because of the withdrawal of American engagement and the specific contingencies of German and French domestic politics.
advanced.theBoundaries: The relationship between the interwar crisis of democracy and the broader phenomenon of democratic backsliding has become a major research area as contemporary anxieties about democracy's resilience have grown. Scholars including Sheri Berman and Anna Grzymala-Busse have analyzed the interwar period comparatively — asking why democracy survived in some countries (Britain, France, Scandinavia) and collapsed in others (Germany, Italy, Spain, Austria, Poland, Hungary). The answers emphasize the interaction of economic crisis, institutional design, political culture, and the specific choices made by political actors at key moments.
advanced.theConditions: The historical debate on the Great Depression has been substantially advanced by economic historians including Barry Eichengreen, Christina Romer, and Ben Bernanke. The Depression is now understood as primarily a monetary phenomenon — the deflationary dynamics of the gold standard — rather than a deep crisis of capitalism as the Marxist tradition argued. This analysis has significant political implications: it suggests that the Depression was not inevitable and that specific policy choices (maintaining gold convertibility) made it worse than it needed to be. The political lesson — that central banks should act aggressively in financial crises rather than defending monetary orthodoxy — was explicitly invoked during the 2008 financial crisis, when Bernanke (then Fed chairman) acted on his academic analysis of the Depression.
advanced.internalDiversity: The global history of the interwar period has received increasing attention. Erez Manela's The Wilsonian Moment shows how the peace settlement disappointed colonial peoples across Asia and Africa, channeling anticolonial nationalism into specific organizational forms. Cemil Aydin's The Politics of Anti-Westernism in Asia traces how pan-Islamic and pan-Asian thought developed as responses to Western imperialism during the interwar period. The interwar period's global dimensions — the Japanese empire, the Chinese revolution, the decolonization movements — are essential for understanding the period as a world crisis rather than simply a European one.
advanced.longConsequences: The Munich comparison has been one of the most consequential and misused analogies in modern political rhetoric. The comparison — drawing lessons from Chamberlain's appeasement of Hitler for contemporary negotiations with adversaries — assumes that the structure of the current situation matches the interwar situation in relevant respects. This assumption is often unexamined and frequently wrong: not every adversary is Hitler, not every negotiation is appeasement, and the costs of military confrontation are not always lower than the costs of negotiation. The misuse of the Munich analogy — most obviously in the case of the 2003 Iraq invasion — illustrates how historical analogies can foreclose thinking rather than illuminate it.
advanced.periodizationDebate: The central confrontation in how historians have read the interwar period runs between accounts that stress deep structural forces (the period's catastrophe was produced by the contradictions of the post-WWI settlement, the logic of capitalism in crisis, or the inherent instability of democratic governance under economic stress) and accounts that stress contingency (the catastrophe resulted from specific policy failures and political choices that could have been made differently). Both positions have generated important scholarship. The force-driven accounts are essential for understanding the conditions within which political actors operated; the contingency accounts are essential for recovering the choices available within those conditions and the moral responsibility of those who made them.

foundation.theStory: The interwar period matters as the context for the 20th century's two greatest catastrophes — the Depression and the Second World War — and as the testing ground for the political, economic, and institutional lessons that shaped the postwar order. It also matters as a period whose apparent inevitability is partly an illusion: recovering the genuine contingency of the interwar period is essential for understanding that political catastrophes are not predetermined and that choices matter.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern the inevitability of the period's catastrophe (structural determination versus contingency), the causes of democratic collapse across most of Europe, the economics of the Depression and its political consequences, and the lessons of appeasement for contemporary international politics.
foundation.whyItMatters: The interwar period matters for understanding the Second World War, the postwar international order, Keynesian economics, the history of fascism and communism, and the relationship between economic crisis and political extremism. Its lessons — about appeasement, economic policy, collective security, and democratic resilience — have been invoked in every subsequent international crisis.
questions: Eric Hobsbawm treated the interwar period as an 'age of catastrophe' in which the contradictions of the post-1919 settlement made fascism and war inevitable. Zara Steiner argues that the 1920s contained genuine possibilities for stability and that the catastrophe resulted from specific policy failures. Which account better fits the historical evidence?

The Munich Agreement of 1938 has become the defining symbol of appeasement — the negotiation that failed to stop Hitler and emboldened him instead. But was appeasement a rational response to genuine constraints, or was it a moral and strategic failure that could have been avoided? And when, if ever, is the Munich analogy appropriately invoked for contemporary situations?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The essential texts are Steiner's two volumes for the diplomatic history, Hobsbawm's Age of Extremes for the structural account, Eichengreen's Golden Fetters for the economic history, and Tooze's Wages of Destruction for the German economic story. For democratic collapse, Sheri Berman's The Primacy of Politics and Juan Linz and Alfred Stepan's comparative work on democratic breakdown are essential. For the cultural history, Modris Eksteins's Rites of Spring provides the most evocative account of the period's intellectual and cultural climate.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidential base is rich across multiple national archives. The primary limits are the incompleteness of archives from the Soviet Union and Eastern European states for parts of the period, the complexity of disaggregating the effects of different causal factors, and the difficulty of counterfactual reasoning about what different policy choices would have produced.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about the interwar period must engage with the structural versus contingency debate and take a position on the degree to which the catastrophe was predetermined. It must address the appeasement question with historical specificity — distinguishing between the constraints that made appeasement understandable and the choices that made it culpable. And it must engage with the economic history — the relationship between the Depression and political extremism — with enough precision to evaluate competing causal claims.
questions: Barry Eichengreen argues that adherence to the gold standard was the primary cause of the Depression's severity and international spread. If this analysis is correct, what does it imply about the responsibility of central bankers and governments for the political consequences of the Depression — including the rise of Hitler?

The interwar period saw democracy collapse across most of Europe while surviving in Britain, France, and Scandinavia. What explains this variation, and what does it suggest about the conditions under which democracies are resilient?

research.topic: The economics of the Great Depression and its international transmission
research.content: The economic history of the Great Depression is well established through the work of Barry Eichengreen (Golden Fetters, 1992), Milton Friedman and Anna Schwartz (A Monetary History of the United States, 1963), and Christina Romer. The Depression is understood as primarily a monetary phenomenon — the gold standard's deflationary dynamics transmitted contractionary shocks across countries and prevented expansionary monetary responses. Countries that left gold early recovered faster. The US Federal Reserve's failure to prevent bank failures and its contraction of the money supply is established as a major cause of the American Depression's severity. Ben Bernanke's academic work on the Depression's transmission through banking failures informed his response as Fed chairman in 2008. The relationship between the Depression and political extremism — the timing of Nazi vote share increase matching German unemployment increase — is documented, though the causal mechanisms are more complex than simple economic determinism.

research.topic: The global history of the interwar period beyond Europe
research.content: The global dimensions of the interwar period have received increasing scholarly attention as the field has moved beyond its traditional European focus. Erez Manela's The Wilsonian Moment established how the 1919 peace settlement's failure to extend self-determination beyond Europe shaped anticolonial nationalism across Asia and Africa. Work on the Japanese empire, Chinese revolution, and Latin American political economy during the Depression has complicated the standard European-centered narrative. The interwar period looks different from the perspective of India, China, or the Middle East than from the perspective of France or Germany — it was a period of anticolonial mobilization, not simply of European democratic failure. This global history has not yet been fully integrated with the dominant European-centered accounts.

research.topic: Whether stable international order was achievable in the 1930s without the Second World War
research.content: What evidence would resolve this: detailed reconstruction of specific decision points — German rearmament (1933–1936), the Rhineland remilitarization (1936), the Anschluss (1938), Munich (1938) — combined with a causal model of how alternative decisions would have interacted with German, Italian, French, British, and Soviet domestic political constraints. Why unavailable: counterfactual historical questions are inherently underdetermined by evidence; the key constraint is that we cannot observe the alternative histories. What can be established: the specific constraints facing policymakers at each decision point; the specific choices made; and the immediate consequences. What cannot be established: whether Hitler's ambitions were satisfiable short of European hegemony — whether any settlement could have produced a stable German acceptance of the post-WWI order — which is the question that determines whether a different policy could have prevented the war.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Failure of Collective Security
comparativeNarrative.content: Liberal internationalists read the interwar period as proof that peace requires enforceable institutions. The League of Nations failed on two counts. It had no power to force its decisions on members. And the United States, its most powerful potential backer, never joined. The lesson was the United Nations and a lasting American commitment to international engagement. What this reading underplays is that interwar diplomacy had real successes: Locarno, the Kellogg-Briand Pact, and the League's resolution of several smaller disputes. The problem was that all those gains rested on goodwill. Goodwill ran out when the aggressive powers of the 1930s stopped pretending.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Depression as Political Cause
comparativeNarrative.content: Historians in the economic tradition argue that the disasters of the 1930s grew mainly from mass unemployment and economic desperation. Ideology mattered less than empty stomachs. The Nazi vote barely moved before 1929. It climbed steeply as German unemployment climbed. That pattern suggests Hitler might never have come to power without the Depression. Better economic policy could have broken the chain: leaving the gold standard earlier, spending more to support demand, and coordinating between countries. This view has a direct message for the present. Economic crisis, not ideology alone, is the greatest threat to democratic government.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Crisis of Liberal Capitalism
comparativeNarrative.content: Marxist thinkers read the interwar period as the moment when free-market capitalism hit its limits. Karl Polanyi's The Great Transformation (1944) is the key text. Polanyi argued that building a self-regulating market in the 19th century had pulled apart older social bonds. The backlash was inevitable. Fascism, on this reading, was capitalism's answer to the threat of socialist revolution. It was a way to defend property and the existing order through authoritarian force. What this view captures is the real link between economic disruption and political violence. What it misses is the role of choice. Specific decisions by politicians and voters turned economic pressure into catastrophe, and those decisions could have gone another way.

rabbitHole.label: The Cold War
rabbitHole.reason: The Cold War emerged directly from the interwar period and the Second World War it produced — the Soviet-American confrontation was shaped by the ideological battles of the 1930s and the wartime alliance that defeated fascism.

rabbitHole.label: The Weimar Republic
rabbitHole.reason: The Weimar Republic is the central case study within the interwar period — its failure is the event through which the interwar crisis of democracy most directly produced catastrophe.

rabbitHole.label: The Treaty of Versailles
rabbitHole.reason: The Treaty of Versailles created the political and economic conditions of the interwar period — its terms, perceived injustices, and enforcement failures are the starting point for understanding everything that followed.

rabbitHole.label: The Second World War
rabbitHole.reason: The Second World War was the interwar period's ultimate consequence — the failure of collective security, democratic resilience, and economic stability across those twenty years produced the most destructive war in human history.

rabbitHole.label: The Great Depression
rabbitHole.reason: The Great Depression was the central event of the interwar period's second decade — the economic catastrophe that destroyed the political stability of the 1920s and created the conditions for fascism's triumph.

rabbitHole.label: The Soviet Union under Stalin
rabbitHole.reason: The Soviet Union's trajectory during the interwar period — forced industrialization, collectivization, the Terror — was one of the three major political experiments of the period alongside liberal democracy and fascism.

rabbitHole.label: Nationalism
rabbitHole.reason: Nationalism — ethnic, economic, and imperial — was the dominant political force of the interwar period, driving both the democratic failures and the fascist movements that exploited those failures.

reference.author: Steiner, Zara
reference.title: The Lights That Failed: European International History 1919–1933
reference.annotation: The essential scholarly account of the 1920s — Steiner recovers the genuine possibilities for stable international order in the decade and analyzes why they were not realized. The first volume of the essential two-volume history.

reference.author: Steiner, Zara
reference.title: The Triumph of the Dark: European International History 1933–1939
reference.annotation: The essential scholarly account of the 1930s — analyzes how Hitler's Germany dismantled the collective security system step by step and why the democracies failed to stop it.

reference.author: Hobsbawm, Eric
reference.title: The Age of Extremes: The Short Twentieth Century 1914–1991
reference.annotation: Hobsbawm's synthesis of 20th-century history treats the interwar period as its central 'age of catastrophe' — the most influential single-volume account, essential despite its structural determinist limitations.

reference.author: Eichengreen, Barry
reference.title: Golden Fetters: The Gold Standard and the Great Depression
reference.annotation: The essential economic history — Eichengreen's argument that the gold standard transmitted deflationary shocks and prevented recovery is the standard account of why the Depression was so severe and so international.

reference.author: Tooze, Adam
reference.title: The Wages of Destruction: The Making and Breaking of the Nazi Economy
reference.annotation: Essential for understanding the economic history of the interwar period through the German economy — Tooze shows why Nazi rearmament was a gamble on conquest and how it shaped Hitler's strategic choices.

reference.author: Cohrs, Patrick O.
reference.title: The Unfinished Peace After World War I: America, Britain and the Stabilisation of Europe, 1919–1932
reference.annotation: Anchors the entry's 'new diplomatic history' claim that the 1920s held genuine, contingent possibilities for a durable peace that failed because of American withdrawal and domestic politics rather than the injustice of Versailles.

reference.author: Manela, Erez
reference.title: The Wilsonian Moment: Self-Determination and the International Origins of Anticolonial Nationalism
reference.annotation: Underpins the entry's global reading of the period, showing how the 1919 settlement disappointed colonial peoples and channeled anticolonial nationalism into new organizational forms.

reference.author: Aydin, Cemil
reference.title: The Politics of Anti-Westernism in Asia: Visions of World Order in Pan-Islamic and Pan-Asian Thought
reference.annotation: Supports the entry's account of the interwar period as a world crisis, tracing how pan-Islamic and pan-Asian thought developed as responses to Western imperialism.

reference.author: Berman, Sheri
reference.title: The Primacy of Politics: Social Democracy and the Making of Europe's Twentieth Century
reference.annotation: Named as an essential text for the comparative analysis of why interwar democracy survived in some states and collapsed in others, emphasizing political agency over structural determination.

reference.author: Linz, Juan J.; Stepan, Alfred (eds.)
reference.title: The Breakdown of Democratic Regimes
reference.annotation: Named as essential comparative scholarship on democratic breakdown, framing the entry's question of why democracy collapsed across most of interwar Europe.

reference.author: Eksteins, Modris
reference.title: Rites of Spring: The Great War and the Birth of the Modern Age
reference.annotation: Named as the most evocative account of the period's intellectual and cultural climate, supporting the entry's cultural-history strand.

reference.author: Polanyi, Karl
reference.title: The Great Transformation: The Political and Economic Origins of Our Time
reference.annotation: The key text the entry narrates for the Marxist/deep-forces reading, arguing that the self-regulating market produced an inevitable social backlash of which fascism was one form.

reference.author: Friedman, Milton; Schwartz, Anna Jacobson
reference.title: A Monetary History of the United States, 1867–1960
reference.annotation: Cited as a pillar of the established monetary reading of the Depression, locating its American severity in Federal Reserve failures and money-supply contraction.

reference.author: Cato (Foot, Michael; Owen, Frank; Howard, Peter)
reference.title: Guilty Men
reference.annotation: The wartime pamphlet the entry treats as the early, damning indictment of Chamberlain's appeasement against which later scholarship reacted.

commerce.title: The Age of Extremes
commerce.author: Eric Hobsbawm
commerce.note: The essential single-volume account — Hobsbawm's sweep and analytical power make this the best starting point despite its structural limitations.

commerce.title: The Lights That Failed
commerce.author: Zara Steiner
commerce.note: The essential scholarly account of the 1920s — recovers the genuine possibilities for stability and why they failed.

commerce.title: The Sleepwalkers
commerce.author: Christopher Clark
commerce.note: Clark's account of how Europe went to war in 1914 is essential context — the war that produced the interwar period.

commerce.title: Homage to Catalonia
commerce.author: George Orwell
commerce.note: Orwell's account of fighting in the Spanish Civil War — the most vivid witness testimony to the interwar period's ideological intensity.

commerce.title: The Wages of Destruction: The Making and Breaking of the Nazi Economy
commerce.author: Adam Tooze
commerce.note: The essential economic history of Nazi Germany and the interwar period — Tooze's account of how Hitler's economic decisions drove German foreign policy is the standard reference for understanding the relationship between economics and the road to war.

popularCulture.title: Cabaret (1972) and The Grand Budapest Hotel (2014)
popularCulture.description: Both films set their stories in these years and show a bright, playful world right up to the moment it shatters. Cabaret puts the nightlife of Weimar Berlin next to growing fear, the two side by side in the same club. Both films make the slide into danger feel sudden. In truth, ordinary people chose to look away slowly, over months and years, as the threat grew.

popularCulture.title: The literature of disillusionment
popularCulture.description: Hemingway's The Sun Also Rises, Remarque's All Quiet on the Western Front, and Woolf's Mrs Dalloway all grew from one wound. The First World War had wrecked the beliefs their writers were raised on, and the books are full of that loss. But these are the voices of people with time and schooling to write. Millions lived through the same years and left no such record.

popularCulture.title: Appeasement as political epithet
popularCulture.description: The Munich Agreement of 1938 gave the word appeasement its modern meaning: giving in to a bully and making things worse. Chamberlain told crowds he had brought home peace for our time. Within a year, the war began. Politicians still reach for Munich as a warning. Sometimes the threat is real. Sometimes the word is used to kill off talks that might have worked.

popularCulture.title: The Great Gatsby as cultural shorthand for the 1920s
popularCulture.description: Fitzgerald's novel is the picture most people reach for when they imagine the 1920s boom. It shows money that feels unreal and a dream that keeps slipping away. But its world is small and rich. Working-class people, immigrants, and Black Americans lived through the same decade, and the novel barely lets them onto the page.

popularCulture.title: Peaky Blinders (2013–2022)
popularCulture.description: This BBC drama follows a Birmingham crime family through these years. It shows how the war left many men scarred, and how that damage drew them toward strongman politics. But the show pins the era's violence on gangsters guarding their turf. The real political violence of the 1930s came from large, organized movements driven by ideas, not from local criminals.

comparativeSummary: Everyone agrees the twenty years between the wars ended in disaster. They disagree about what drove it, and the answer carries a charge: was the catastrophe made by people, or forced by pressures larger than any decision? One reading blames fragile institutions and the goodwill that ran out when aggressors stopped pretending. Another points to mass unemployment, arguing empty stomachs, not ideas, put Hitler in power. A third sees the market economy reaching its limits, with fascism as capitalism's violent defense. Where you place the blame decides how much room you leave for choice, and whom you hold responsible.
